title: Corticosteroid Therapy
subtitle: The nurse manages corticosteroids — never stopping them abruptly, giving with food, and watching for high glucose, infection risk, fluid retention, and Cushingoid effects.

patient.name: Corticosteroids
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely administers and monitors corticosteroid therapy.

labs.v: Moon face, buffalo hump, weight gain.
Mood changes, slow healing, GI ulcer.

nursesNote: The nurse safely administers corticosteroids (such as prednisone, hydrocortisone, methylprednisolone), which reduce inflammation and suppress the immune response across many conditions. The most critical teaching is to never stop the drug abruptly: long-term use suppresses the adrenal glands, and sudden withdrawal can trigger acute adrenal insufficiency (Addisonian crisis), so the dose is tapered slowly. The drug is given with food or milk to protect the stomach, since it raises the risk of GI ulcers. Corticosteroids raise blood glucose, so the nurse monitors sugar levels, especially in patients with diabetes, and they suppress immunity and can mask the usual signs of infection (including fever), so infection risk is high and sick contacts are avoided. Sodium and water retention can cause weight gain, edema, and higher blood pressure, while potassium tends to fall. Long-term effects include osteoporosis (calcium and vitamin D are encouraged), and Cushingoid changes such as a moon face, buffalo hump, and central weight gain. The nurse also watches for mood changes, delayed wound healing, and bruising, teaches the patient to carry medical identification, and reports signs of infection promptly.
providerOrders: Teach the patient to never stop corticosteroids abruptly and to taper as ordered
Give the dose with food or milk to protect the stomach
Monitor blood glucose, especially in patients with diabetes
Assess for infection and teach the patient to avoid sick contacts
Monitor weight, edema, blood pressure, and potassium

opts.text: They reduce inflammation and immune response
They should never be stopped abruptly
They are taken with food to protect the stomach
They lower blood glucose
They increase the risk of infection
They can cause sodium and water retention
Abruptly stopping can cause adrenal crisis
Steroids can mask the usual signs of infection
Potassium tends to rise on steroids
Long-term use raises osteoporosis risk
Glucose should be monitored, especially in diabetics
Cushingoid changes include a moon face

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order starting a patient on long-term prednisone

opts.text: Verify the order and baseline glucose and weight
Give the dose with food
Teach to never stop the drug abruptly
Teach infection precautions and reporting
Monitor glucose, weight, and blood pressure

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Expected effect or Reason to report?

opts.text: Slightly higher blood glucose readings
Fever, sore throat, or cough
Mild increase in appetite
Black, tarry stools
Gradual central weight gain over weeks
Sudden weakness after missing several doses

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A long-term steroid patient stopped the drug and is weak and hypotensive
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess vital signs and level of consciousness
Suspect adrenal crisis from abrupt withdrawal
Notify the provider urgently
Give IV fluids and corticosteroid as ordered
Monitor response and document
Tapering the dose slowly as ordered
Stopping the steroid as soon as feeling better
Taking the dose with food or milk
Visiting friends who are sick with the flu
Monitoring blood glucose at home
Ignoring a low-grade fever as harmless
Carrying medical identification about steroid use
Adding extra salt to every meal
